message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Commit often and monitor CI/CD">
Project owner directive: **Commit early and often.** Whenever you make a change and the tests pass (cargo fmt, cargo clippy, cargo test all green), commit it immediately. Don't batch up large changes — small, frequent commits. This applies to all work: code, CSS, docs, migrations, everything.

Also: **monitor CI/CD workflows.** After pushing, check that the GitHub Actions pipeline passes (cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test). If CI fails, fix it before moving on. Use `gh run list` and `gh run view` to check workflow status.

This is a non-negotiable practice going forward. Small commits, green CI, always.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Understood. I have uncommitted CSS work in `static/css/main.css`. Let me check the current git status and get this committed.

content.text:
There's a lot of uncommitted work from multiple teammates. My CSS file is in `static/css/main.css` (untracked under `static/`). There's also Luca's scaffolding work (Cargo.toml, src/, tests/, migrations, docker-compose, config, templates, etc.).

I should only commit my own work. Let me check what's specifically mine versus what others are working on. The `.env` file should NOT be committed (likely contains secrets).

My work is just `static/css/main.css`. The rest of the untracked files are from Luca's scaffolding. I should commit only my file. But first -- the CI pipeline runs `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy`, and `cargo test`. Since my change is only a CSS file, it won't affect those checks. But there might not be a compilable project to test against without Luca's files.

Let me check if there's already been a commit from Luca that I need to be aware of, and whether the project compiles.

Luca's scaffolding is here but uncommitted. I shouldn't commit the full project on his behalf -- that's his work. But I can commit just my CSS file. A CSS-only commit won't break CI since the previous commit's Rust code (which had no src/) wouldn't have cargo tests that fail.

Actually, wait -- looking at this more carefully, the branch is already 1 commit ahead of origin (the docs commit). CI currently runs cargo commands. If I commit just a CSS file, CI will try to run `cargo fmt --check` etc. on whatever Rust code exists at HEAD. Let me check if the CI workflow will even work without Cargo.toml at HEAD.

content.content:
1→name: CI
     2→
     3→on:
     4→  push:
     5→    branches: [main]
     6→  pull_request:
     7→    branches: [main]
     8→
     9→env:
    10→  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always
    11→
    12→jobs:
    13→  check:
    14→    name: Check
    15→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    16→    steps:
    17→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    18→
    19→      - name: Install Rust nightly
    20→        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly
    21→        with:
    22→          components: rustfmt, clippy
    23→
    24→      - name: Cache cargo registry and build
    25→        uses: actions/cache@v4
    26→        with:
    27→          path: |
    28→            ~/.cargo/registry
    29→            ~/.cargo/git
    30→            target
    31→          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}
    32→          restore-keys: |
    33→            ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-
    34→
    35→      - name: Check formatting
    36→        run: cargo fmt --check
    37→
    38→      - name: Run clippy
    39→        run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    40→
    41→      - name: Run tests
    42→        run: cargo test
    43→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always

jobs:
  check:
    name: Check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Rust nightly
        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly
        with:
          components: rustfmt, clippy

      - name: Cache cargo registry and build
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.cargo/registry
            ~/.cargo/git
            target
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}
          restore-keys: |
            ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-

      - name: Check formatting
        run: cargo fmt --check

      - name: Run clippy
        run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings

      - name: Run tests
        run: cargo test

content.text:
The CI workflow runs cargo commands. Without a `Cargo.toml` at HEAD, CI would fail. The previous commit that was pushed doesn't have Cargo.toml, so CI is probably already failing or has never run with cargo successfully.

The right approach is: Luca's scaffolding and my CSS should both be committed. Since the team lead said "commit early and often" and we're all working on main, I should commit my CSS work now. If Luca's work isn't committed yet, CI will still fail on cargo commands -- but that's not something I can fix from the CSS side.

Let me just commit my CSS file. It's clean, reviewed by the team, and Task #3 is done.

input.description: Stage the CSS design tokens file
Verify only the CSS file is staged

content.text: Good -- only my CSS file is staged. Let me commit it.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add CSS design tokens and base styles foundation

Establishes the complete CSS architecture with cascade layers
(reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities). Design tokens
encode Steve Schoger's visual specs: spacing scale (4px base),
oklch color palette, fluid type scale, shadows, radii, and
transitions. Includes accessibility foundations per Heydon's review:
double-ring focus-visible, forced-colors support, prefers-reduced-motion,
and visually-hidden utility.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text: Commit successful. My CSS is committed at `06c8ee0`. The branch is 2 commits ahead of origin. I won't push yet -- the remaining untracked files (Cargo.toml, src/, etc.) are Luca's scaffolding work that needs to be committed first for CI to pass. Pushing just my CSS commit would trigger a CI run that fails on cargo commands since Cargo.toml isn't in the commit history yet.

input.content:
CSS design tokens committed: `06c8ee0` -- "Add CSS design tokens and base styles foundation"

I'm holding off on pushing because the branch is 2 commits ahead of origin and neither commit includes `Cargo.toml` / `src/`. Pushing now would trigger CI which would fail on `cargo fmt --check` since there's no Rust project in the commit history yet. 

Luca's scaffolding work (Cargo.toml, src/, tests/, migrations, docker-compose, config, templates) is all still untracked. Once Luca commits his work and we push together, CI should be green. Alternatively, we could push all uncommitted work as one coordinated push.

Also noting: there's an `.env` file in the root that should NOT be committed -- it likely contains database credentials. Should be added to `.gitignore`.
